availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.p:
Again Sepulveda in confirmation of his error notes another word from
the same bull of Alexander VI in which the pontiff says that he desires nothing more than the exaltation of the glory of Christ, and that the barbarians be suppressed. This last he hurls at us as an Achillean argument, Sepulveda: yet if the whole sequence of the bull be read, it will be manifestly clear that this is their calumny, not to say shamelessness. The matter stands thus: the pontiff praises the zeal of the Catholic kings, and with much verbal honor recounts the victory of the Kingdom of Granada, having defeated the savage and barbarous enemy, most famous throughout the whole world, in these words: Wherefore when we were called to this See of Peter, we recognized you, knowing that you have always been, as true Catholic kings and princes, and your glorious deeds,
already known to nearly the whole world, demonstrate: not only to exhort this, but with all effort, zeal and diligence, no labors. And below: just as the recovery of the Kingdom of Granada from the tyranny of the Saracens in our times made by you with such great glory of the divine name testifies.

From these words it is elicited that the words of the pontiff saying that he desires nothing more than to see the barbarians suppressed, refer to the Moors of Granada, who are barbarians as I said above, and to other barbarians, most hostile enemies of the Christian name. Therefore those words of the bull do nothing to confirm that impious opinion. For how could the Roman pontiff have approved what is most distant from the doctrine of Christ, as has been sufficiently argued above?

These things I have had to put forward, according to the measure of grace granted to me, in defense of this most holy cause, joined with Christian piety. As for what remains, I exhort and warn brother Sepulveda and my colleague in Christ and the other enemies of the Indians, through Jesus Christ, that they obey the words of the Lord and listen to and revere the traditions of the holy fathers, and fear God, the avenger of perverse machinations. The Indians are our brothers, for whom Christ gave his life: why do we persecute them with such immense cruelty, and warlike apparatus
when they deserve no such thing?
Since itos what is
done cannot be undone, let them be attributed to our weakness, / so long as there is restitution of what was impiously seized.

Henceforth, however, let there be no cruelty and warlike apparatus, more suited to Mohammedans than to Christians. Let upright preachers be sent to them, who express Jesus Christ in their conduct and reflect the spirit of Peter and Paul. I know well that they will embrace evangelical doctrine. For they are not stupid or barbarous, but with a certain innate sincerity, simple, modest, / gentle, finally such that I do not know whether there is any nation more suited to receiving the gospel. Once they have received it, it is wonderful with what piety, what ardor, / what faith and charity they fulfill the precepts of Christ and venerate the sacraments. For they are teachable and talented, and in industry and natural gifts they surpass many nations of the known world, which the second part of this our apology, written in Spanish, will place before everyone's eyes with the clearest arguments, examples, and a true description of that world, so that henceforth those most impious plunderers may be silent, who with venomous slanders and criminal lies have defamed a people most sincere of all, / teachable, modest and talented.
